options.label: Teacher
School Leader
District Leader
Technology Leader
Instructional Coach
Other

scoring.rule: Add the selected option's weight to its character_id. The character with the highest total is the final result.
scoring.tie_breaker: If two or more characters tie, select the character associated with the latest answer among the tied characters.

nova.name: Nova the Star
nova.summary: Your energy brings people together and makes learning unforgettable.

axel.name: Axel the Racer
axel.summary: You turn competition into motivation and pressure into progress.

pip.name: Pip the Explorer
pip.summary: You notice the small details and connect patterns with ease.

mugsy.name: Mugsy the Caretaker
mugsy.summary: You hold the group together, turning uncertainty into trust and teamwork.

cherry_lu.name: Cherry Lu the Singer
cherry_lu.summary: You are a fearless strategist who transforms chaos into opportunity.

tico.name: Tico the Surfer
tico.summary: You are drawn to new ideas, always searching for the next big wave.

bobbi.name: Bobbi the Scout
bobbi.summary: You believe mastery takes time, care, and steady progress.

marsha.name: Marsha the Artist
marsha.summary: You raise the bar for everyone, showing what focus and expertise can do.

questions.question: It's the night before a big lesson and it won't work for three of your students. You:

options.label: Reach out to a colleague — another perspective usually unlocks it.
Redesign it. The clock is ticking and you're not backing down.
Research until you find the exact reason it's not working.
Simplify for everyone and check in with those three first thing.
Map two parallel tracks and execute both.
Pivot completely — a new path usually works better than fixing the old one.
Pull from your organized resources and rebuild from solid ground.
Stay up until every version is exactly right.

questions.question: Your lesson is five minutes in and you can feel it's not landing. You:

options.label: Get everyone talking to see if they can solve the lesson together.
Push through and pick up the pace — momentum matters more than the plan.
Figure out the why before you change a single thing.
Check in with the class — something might be going on beyond the lesson itself.
Move to your backup plan — you always have one ready.
Throw out the plan entirely and try something different.
Go back to the last point where everyone was with you and rebuild from there.
Won't move forward until everyone understands the lesson.

questions.question: The thing that exhausts you most as a teacher is:

options.label: Bringing the energy every day and rarely getting that energy back.
Watching capable students coast when you know they could do better.
Sitting through meetings that cover what you already figured out on your own.
Pouring everything into your students and having nothing left for yourself.
Watching others ignore approaches that clearly get results.
Being underestimated because your approach doesn't look conventional.
Being pushed to move on before the foundation is solid.
Sharing work before it's finished.

questions.question: A student is struggling and falling behind. Your instinct is to:

options.label: Find a small win and make it visible — sometimes confidence is the real gap.
Set a clear challenge goal — a concrete target can shift everything.
Figure out where they're getting stuck before doing anything.
Check in privately about how they're doing beyond the work.
Build a targeted plan and track their progress every week.
Change the approach if the current one isn't working.
Break the concept into smaller pieces and rebuild from the ground up.
Build a thorough re-teaching plan before doing anything else.

questions.question: When you picture the teacher you most want to be, you see:

options.label: The one students still talk about decades later because of how fun class was.
The one who pushed them further than they thought they could go.
The one who gave them a lifelong love of asking why.
The one who never gave up on a struggling student.
The one whose students always showed up prepared.
The one who taught them to take risks and find their own way.
The one who built the strong foundations.
The one whose standards made students exceed their own expectations.

questions.question: Test scores came back and one grade level is significantly behind. Your first move:

options.label: Rally the staff — belief and momentum come before strategy.
Set a concrete target and hold everyone accountable from day one.
Take time to study the data to find a pattern.
Meet with those teachers privately — they need support, not blame.
Build an intervention plan with clear ownership and a deadline.
Ask whether there's an approach the team hasn't tried yet.
Check the instructional fundamentals before assuming anything.
Create a detailed improvement plan that leaves no variable unaddressed.

questions.question: When you walk into a classroom, the first thing you notice is:

options.label: Does the room feel alive or like everyone is waiting for the bell?
Is the lesson rigorous enough?
Who's engaging, who isn't, and why.
Which students look lost or like they've already given up.
Whether the objective is clear and the lesson is on track.
Is this the best method to teach the lesson?
Does everyone understand the basics?
What's missing and how far off it is.

questions.question: Your biggest leadership challenge right now is:

options.label: Keeping staff energized when burnout is everywhere.
Getting teachers to move faster and stop resisting change.
Translating data insights into something teachers can use.
Supporting my team's wellbeing while the district keeps adding demands.
Getting buy-in for strategies I already know will work.
Being taken seriously when I challenge the way things have always been done.
Defending slow, foundational change when everyone wants a quick win.
Sharing systems before they're fully refined. The risk feels too high.

questions.question: A teacher is resistant to a new instructional initiative. You:

options.label: Connect it to something bigger — when people believe in the why, the how gets easier.
Be direct — the expectation is clear and this isn't optional.
Ask questions to find where the resistance is coming from.
Make sure they know you're in their corner — resistance usually comes from fear.
Show them the data and make the cost of staying stuck impossible to ignore.
Ask what it would take to get them on board — there might be a better path.
Break it into smaller steps so it doesn't feel like everything is changing at once.
Give them every resource and model they need — preparation removes resistance.

questions.question: The thing you most want your school to be known for is:

options.label: A place where students and staff genuinely want to be.
A place that gets results because we never let comfort beat growth.
A place that catches problems early because we pay attention.
A place where no student slips through the cracks.
A place where every plan is executed to reach our goals.
A place that's always asking what's possible instead of defending what's familiar.
A place built carefully enough to hold up when everything gets hard.
A place where the standard is set high and held consistently.

questions.question: A new state mandate drops with 90-day implementation. Your response:

options.label: Host a launch event to build energy and buy-in before the details land.
Assign owners, set milestones, and schedule a progress check in 30 days.
Read every word of the policy before I schedule any meetings.
Call my principals first to understand what capacity actually exists.
Draft a rollout strategy with risk mitigation built in from the start.
Find the fastest path forward that doesn't compromise what's already working.
Assess the current situation before committing to any implementation path.
Build documentation thorough enough to answer every question before it gets asked.

questions.question: When a major decision needs to be made fast, your instinct is:

options.label: Get the right people in a room and build momentum around a direction.
Move quickly, decide, and adjust if needed.
Slow down and gather more information before anyone commits to anything.
Check in with the people closest to the impact before committing to a direction.
Map the options, assess the risk, and move with a clear rationale.
Ask whether the urgency is real or just pressure — sometimes the right move is to reframe.
Review what's already in place before deciding what needs to change.
Document the decision criteria clearly so the outcome can be defended later.

questions.question: The district-level challenge keeping you up at night is:

options.label: Getting schools genuinely inspired by the vision — not just compliant with it.
Too many initiatives sitting still when urgency is clearly needed.
Making evidence-based decisions in a highly political environment.
Supporting principals who are burning out under impossible demands.
Watching the right strategy stall because I couldn't get buy-in fast enough.
Legacy systems and collective fear of change slowing everything down.
Pressure to show fast wins before the foundational work is finished.
Launching anything before it's been fully vetted. At this level, the stakes don't allow for it.

questions.question: A school in your district is consistently underperforming. You:

options.label: I visit in person and make a public commitment to the community.
I set turnaround metrics and build a support plan around the principal.
I study the data patterns for root cause before making any move.
I check in with the principal, teachers, admins, and staff first.
I bring in my best people, build a clear strategy, and execute it.
I push the team to break from the model that clearly isn't working.
I run a systems audit before changing any programs or people.
I build a comprehensive turnaround plan with documented benchmarks for every stage.

questions.question: In 10 years, you want your district known as the place that:

options.label: Made every student feel like the future was built for them.
Raised the bar so high it made every other district reconsider their ceiling.
Figured out what actually works before anyone else did.
Put students and educators first — always, no exceptions.
Made bold bets that transformed outcomes at a scale that mattered.
Kept asking what's possible until it found a way that worked better than anything before it.
Built the most sustainable, replicable systems in the state.
Set the gold standard for every policy, program, and practice.

questions.question: A new ed-tech platform is approved district-wide. Rollout is in 6 weeks. You:

options.label: I kick off with a demo event — make it feel exciting before it feels mandatory.
I build a hard timeline, assign trainers by school, and track compliance.
I spend two weeks testing every edge case teachers will eventually hit.
I survey teachers first — adoption lives or dies on their readiness.
I map the full strategy with contingencies before anyone trains anyone.
I run a small pilot, learn fast, and iterate before full rollout.
I build the infrastructure and documentation library before anything else.
I build a master playbook and won't hand it to a trainer until every question has an answer.

questions.question: When choosing between two technology solutions, the factor that matters most is:

options.label: Which one teachers and students will actually want to use.
Which one has the strongest track record of moving outcomes.
Which one I've had enough time to properly evaluate end to end.
Which one was built with the end user genuinely in mind.
Which one fits the long-term strategy — not just the current moment.
Which one opens up something that wasn't possible before.
Which one has the infrastructure and support structure to actually hold up.
Which one fully meets every compliance, security, and documentation standard.

questions.question: The tech leadership challenge I face most often is:

options.label: Getting teachers genuinely excited rather than passively tolerating new tools.
Slow adoption when the ROI is sitting right there in plain sight.
Being asked to recommend tools before I've had time to properly evaluate them.
Technology being deployed without asking whether teachers are actually ready.
Leadership choosing flashy tools over ones that are strategically sound.
Being treated like a gadget-chaser instead of a strategic innovator.
Being pushed to deploy before the systems and support structures are ready.
Releasing solutions before they're fully optimized.

questions.question: Teachers report students are disengaged with the learning platform. You:

options.label: I organize a student showcase to make the platform feel worth using.
I pull the usage data and set a concrete re-engagement goal with the team.
I interview teachers and students to find the exact friction points.
I meet with the teachers — the engagement problem usually starts there.
I audit whether the platform is being used strategically or just as busywork.
I ask whether a completely different tool — or no tool — would serve better.
I run a full implementation audit before changing the platform or the approach.
I pull detailed usage reports and error logs before drawing any conclusions.

questions.question: I measure technology success in a school or district by:

options.label: How many people are talking about it positively — buzz is a real signal.
Measurable improvement in academic outcomes that can be tied to the tool.
Whether it genuinely changed how students think and approach learning.
Whether teachers and students feel supported by it, not overwhelmed.
ROI: cost, time saved, and performance gains across the whole system.
Whether it opened up learning pathways that simply didn't exist before.
Uptime, reliability, and whether the infrastructure held up under real use.
Clean data, zero incidents, and full compliance with every benchmark.

questions.question: You're asked to coach a teacher whose lesson engagement is tanking. First step:

options.label: I invite them to co-teach with me — energy and modeling work better than advice.
I set a clear, measurable goal together and build in accountability checkpoints.
I observe silently for a full week before I say a single word.
I start by asking how they're really doing — trust has to come before feedback.
I map the gaps, design a targeted intervention, and bring the evidence.
I ask if they'd be open to trying something completely different — sometimes a reset works better than a fix.
I walk through their lesson plan to find the structural gaps before anything else.
I build a comprehensive coaching plan before our first formal conversation.

questions.question: You know a coaching cycle is going well when:

options.label: The teacher's energy in the classroom has visibly shifted.
Progress is measurable and moving faster than expected.
The teacher is asking questions they weren't asking six weeks ago.
The teacher is trusting the process instead of bracing for judgment.
Every milestone is hitting on schedule and the data is moving.
The teacher is trying things they would have dismissed at the start.
The foundational shifts are holding and showing up consistently.
Every session is documented and the growth is clearly evidenced.

questions.question: The part of instructional support work that drains me most is:

options.label: Doing the behind-the-scenes work that moves everything but gets no recognition.
Coaching teachers who aren't willing to be coached.
Being pressured to prescribe solutions before I've had time to diagnose.
Carrying the emotional weight of every teacher and student I support.
Watching schools leave results on the table by not using frameworks that clearly work.
Being dismissed as too experimental when my methods actually get results.
Curriculum being rushed to rollout before the foundation is fully built.
Releasing resources before every detail is refined.

questions.question: A school asks you to design a new curriculum unit in two weeks. You:

options.label: I get teachers excited about co-creating it — their energy shapes the outcome.
I break it into daily deliverables and hit every deadline on the dot.
I research extensively before writing a single standard or objective.
I build in teacher voice from day one — ownership drives adoption.
I design the assessment first and work backwards from the end goal.
I treat the constraint as a creative challenge — tight timelines sharpen my thinking.
I insist on a clear scope and sequence before drafting a single lesson.
I push back on the timeline. Quality cannot be negotiated.

questions.question: When I picture my highest-impact work, it looks like:

options.label: A room full of teachers who are genuinely excited about teaching again.
A team that hit a goal they genuinely didn't believe was possible six months ago.
A breakthrough insight that permanently shifted how a teacher sees their students.
A teacher who came back from the edge of leaving the profession.
A curriculum framework still being used at scale three years after I built it.
A teacher who finally broke free from the only way they'd ever taught.
A coherent, well-sequenced curriculum that every teacher can actually use.
A lesson that executed flawlessly because every single element was precisely planned.

questions.question: A new education policy just dropped and everyone in the room has a different opinion about what it means. You:

options.label: I get the room talking — shared energy leads to shared understanding.
I cut through the noise and focus on what we actually need to do by Monday.
I go straight to the source and read what it actually says — I'm not forming an opinion on a summary.
I check in on how people are feeling before jumping to what it means.
I figure out who the key decision-makers are and position accordingly.
I ask what this opens up that wasn't possible before.
I map out what systems will be affected before anyone starts reacting.
I'm already running the scenarios in my head — every way this could play out before I say a word.

questions.question: You're three days into a new consulting or research engagement. You're spending most of your time:

options.label: Building relationships and getting the room energized around the work.
Identifying the highest-leverage problems and moving toward them fast.
Gathering context, reading documents, and forming your own view before engaging.
Listening to understand what people are actually experiencing.
Mapping stakeholders, pressure points, and positioning before making any moves.
Looking for the angle or insight others in the room haven't found yet.
Understanding the existing systems and structures before recommending anything.
Documenting everything so the work can be built upon and defended.

questions.question: The thing that frustrates you most about the education and technology space is:

options.label: Good ideas dying because nobody made them feel exciting or urgent enough.
Slow-moving institutions that resist change even when students are falling behind.
Decisions being made without data — or worse, with the wrong data.
Solutions designed without ever truly listening to the people they're meant to help.
Politics and optics getting in the way of what everyone knows needs to happen.
The field defaulting to the same tired approaches when better ones clearly exist.
Innovation being celebrated before anyone checks whether the foundation can hold it.
Work getting released or funded before it's actually ready.

questions.question: You're collaborating with a team and it becomes clear everyone has a different idea of what success looks like. You:

options.label: I get everyone talking and find the shared vision.
I push to define success with a specific, measurable outcome before we go any further.
I ask everyone to write their definition independently before anyone influences anyone else.
I make sure every voice in the room feels heard before we try to align.
I take the lead on defining it — ambiguity is a risk I'm not willing to carry.
I reframe the question — maybe we don't all need the same definition to move forward.
I draft a shared framework and bring it back to the group for validation.
I won't move forward until we've agreed on exactly what we're aiming for.

questions.question: When you think about the impact you most want your work to have on students and schools, it's:

options.label: I want students to feel like the future of education was built with them in mind.
I want to show that urgency and high expectations are how you grow — not things to fear.
I want to surface the truth about what's working so better decisions get made.
I want every educator I work with to feel less alone and more capable than before.
I want to build something that moves the needle at scale — not just in one classroom.
I want to open a door to a way of doing things nobody had seriously considered yet.
I want to leave behind something so well-built it keeps working long after I'm gone.
I want my work to set a standard the field actually rises to meet.
